skill.name: k8s-security-policies
skill.description: Guide Kubernetes security policy design with NetworkPolicy, Pod Security Standards, RBAC, admission controls, and service mesh security examples.
skill.summary: Design and review Kubernetes security policies for workload isolation, least privilege, and compliance controls.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution and weak cryptography patterns, but review found these are Markdown and YAML examples rather than executable skill code. The real risk is semantic: several examples grant broad RBAC permissions or broad external egress, which could weaken a Kubernetes cluster if copied without narrowing. No evidence found of prompt injection, malicious intent, credential theft, or hidden data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Over-Permissive RBAC Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes wildcard RBAC permissions and a cluster-wide secret reader example. These are legitimate Kubernetes examples, but they are risky defaults for production because they can grant broad administrative or secret access if copied directly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited YAML grants wildcard resources and verbs or cluster-wide secret read permissions. The context is instructional rather than malicious, so the finding is medium risk instead of high.

medium_findings.title: Broad External Egress Template
medium_findings.description: The NetworkPolicy template permits HTTPS egress to 0.0.0.0/0 while excluding the instance metadata address. Blocking metadata is positive, but the default still allows broad outbound internet access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The policy explicitly allows all IPv4 destinations on TCP 443, with only the metadata service excluded. This is a clear broad egress pattern, although it may be legitimate for some workloads.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged fenced Markdown blocks and inline command examples as shell execution. These are not executed by the skill, but users should treat the kubectl commands as examples to run only in the intended cluster context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is inside Markdown code fences and inline documentation, with no script file or runtime invocation path. The remaining risk is user misuse of copied administrative commands.

low_findings.title: Missing Referenced Playbook File
low_findings.description: The skill instructs users to open a resources playbook, but that file is not present in the audited file structure. This is not malicious, but it can reduce reliability and leave users without expected implementation detail.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced path appears in the skill instructions, and the generated file structure includes only SKILL.md, assets/network-policy-template.yaml, and references/rbac-patterns.md. This supports a reliability finding, not a security block.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wildcard RBAC Permissions
dangerous_patterns.description: Wildcard resources and verbs can grant namespace-wide administrative authority. Use only when the role is intentionally scoped and reviewed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in the RBAC example and is explicitly dangerous in production. The same file later warns against wildcard permissions, which lowers concern about malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cluster-Wide Secret Read Permissions
dangerous_patterns.description: A ClusterRole that can list and watch secrets can expose sensitive data across namespaces. Prefer namespace-scoped Roles and resourceNames where possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example grants access to secrets at ClusterRole scope. It is presented as a configuration pattern, so the concern is unsafe guidance rather than confirmed abuse.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad HTTPS Egress
dangerous_patterns.description: Allowing egress to 0.0.0.0/0 on HTTPS can permit unintended outbound communication. Restrict egress to known CIDRs, namespaces, or approved service endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The policy clearly allows all IPv4 destinations on TCP 443 while excluding only metadata. This is a common operational pattern, but it is too broad for strict isolation.

content.value_statement: Kubernetes security policy design is complex and easy to over-permit. This skill helps teams draft and review NetworkPolicy, RBAC, Pod Security Standards, admission controls, and service mesh policies.
actual_capabilities: Explains Kubernetes Pod Security Standards and namespace enforcement labels.
Provides NetworkPolicy examples for default deny, DNS, ingress, monitoring, database, and cross-namespace traffic.
Shows RBAC patterns for read-only access, namespace administration, deployment management, and service accounts.
Includes security context examples for non-root containers, dropped capabilities, seccomp, and read-only filesystems.
Covers OPA Gatekeeper constraints and Istio mTLS authorization policy examples.
Lists troubleshooting commands for NetworkPolicy and RBAC permission checks.
limitations: Examples require review before use in production clusters.
The skill does not inspect a live Kubernetes cluster by itself.
Some examples use broad permissions to demonstrate patterns.
A referenced implementation playbook is not included in the audited files.

use_cases.title: Design namespace isolation
use_cases.description: Create default-deny and allow-list NetworkPolicies for application namespaces.

use_cases.title: Review least-privilege access
use_cases.description: Compare RBAC roles against workload needs and reduce wildcard or secret access.

use_cases.title: Prepare compliance controls
use_cases.description: Map Pod Security Standards, audit logging, and admission controls to cluster hardening goals.

prompt_templates.title: Create a default deny baseline
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a default-deny NetworkPolicy baseline for the namespace named staging. Include DNS access only.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a workload manifest
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Kubernetes pod specification and suggest Pod Security Standards changes for restricted mode.

prompt_templates.title: Reduce RBAC permissions
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these RBAC roles and bindings. Recommend least-privilege changes and identify wildcard or secret access risks.

prompt_templates.title: Design multi-tenant controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Kubernetes security policy set for three tenant namespaces with ingress isolation, approved egress, RBAC boundaries, and admission control checks.

output_examples.input: Request for a production namespace network baseline.
output_examples.output: A policy plan with default deny, DNS allowance, application-to-database traffic, ingress-controller access, and verification steps.

output_examples.input: RBAC review for a service account used by a deployment pipeline.
output_examples.output: A least-privilege recommendation that removes wildcard verbs, scopes access to one namespace, and separates deployment permissions from secret access.

output_examples.input: Compliance hardening request for application pods.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering restricted Pod Security Standards, non-root execution, read-only filesystems, dropped capabilities, and audit logging.

best_practices: Start with deny-by-default network policies and add explicit allow rules.
Use namespace-scoped Roles before ClusterRoles whenever possible.
Review generated policies against real workload traffic and compliance requirements.
anti_patterns: Do not copy wildcard RBAC examples into production without strict scope review.
Do not allow 0.0.0.0/0 egress when approved endpoints are known.
Do not bind sensitive permissions to the default service account.

faq.question: Does this skill apply policies to my cluster?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. You must review and apply changes with your own Kubernetes tooling.

faq.question: Can it help with Pod Security Standards?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains privileged, baseline, and restricted namespace labels and related pod security context settings.

faq.question: Does it include NetworkPolicy templates?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes templates for default deny, DNS, ingress, monitoring, external HTTPS, database access, and cross-namespace traffic.

faq.question: Are the RBAC examples production ready?
faq.answer: Not always. Some examples demonstrate broad access and must be narrowed before production use.

faq.question: Can it replace a Kubernetes security audit?
faq.answer: No. It can support review, but it does not replace cluster-specific testing or expert assessment.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
